source_paper_title: New Combinatorial Insights for Monotone Apportionment

Section 6 (Discussion), page 23.

Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
title: Achieve quota and ex-ante proportionality under population monotonicity with fixed house size
background_latex:
Let there be \(n\) states with strictly positive integer populations \(p=(p_1,\dots,p_n)\in\mathbb{N}^n\). Fix a house size \(H\in\mathbb{N}\), and write the total population as \(P=\sum_{i=1}^n p_i\). The (fractional) proportional share, or quota, of state \(i\) is \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{P}H\in\mathbb{R}_+\).

An (integer) apportionment vector is \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i=H\).

A (possibly randomized) apportionment method with fixed house size outputs, for each instance \((p,H)\), a random apportionment vector \(X(p,H)\in\mathbb{N}_0^n\) satisfying \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i(p,H)=H\) almost surely.

The method is quota-compliant if for every instance \((p,H)\) and every state \(i\), \(X_i(p,H)\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely.

The method is ex-ante proportional if for every instance \((p,H)\) and every state \(i\), \(\mathbb{E}[X_i(p,H)]=q_i\).

The method is population-monotone if it never assigns strictly fewer seats to a state \(i\) and strictly more to another state \(j\) when the population ratio changes in favor of \(i\). Concretely: for any two instances \((p,H)\) and \((p',H')\), any two states \(i,j\in[n]\) with \(p'_i/p'_j\ge p_i/p_j\), it is not the case that \(X_i(p,H)>X_i(p',H')\) and \(X_j(p,H)<X_j(p',H')\) with positive probability (equivalently, population monotonicity holds almost surely for every realization of the underlying random choice of method, in the paper's model).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method with fixed house size such that for every instance \((p,H)\):

1) (Fixed house size) \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i(p,H)=H\) almost surely.

2) (Quota compliance) For every \(i\in[n]\), \(X_i(p,H)\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely, where \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{\sum_{j=1}^n p_j}H\).

3) (Ex-ante proportionality) For every \(i\in[n]\), \(\mathbb{E}[X_i(p,H)]=q_i\).

4) (Population monotonicity) The method satisfies population monotonicity as defined above.

Equivalently, either construct such a method or prove that no such method exists.

The method is quota-compliant if for every instance \((p,H)\) and every state \(i\), \(X_i(p,H)\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely.

Determine whether there exists a randomized apportionment method with fixed house size such that for every instance \((p,H)\):

1) (Fixed house size) \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i(p,H)=H\) almost surely.

2) (Quota compliance) For every \(i\in[n]\), \(X_i(p,H)\in\{\lfloor q_i\rfloor,\lceil q_i\rceil\}\) almost surely, where \(q_i=\frac{p_i}{\sum_{j=1}^n p_j}H\).

open_problem_quote: Whether a similar result holds without violating the house size is open.
subject_classification: social choice and apportionment theory (randomized rounding under monotonicity axioms)
counterexample_suitability_rationale: If the conjectured existence is false, counterexamples might be found by searching over small \(n\), \(H\), and population vectors, but the space of randomized mechanisms is enormous and requires specifying a restricted parametric family or an impossibility certificate to test automatically.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Automatically evaluating candidate mechanisms would require a precise, finite representation of a mechanism and an oracle to verify population monotonicity, quota, and exact house size on all instances, which is not straightforward without restricting the mechanism class or proving structural characterizations.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
